ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for salted egg yolk cheese dry noodles specifically. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for salted egg yolk cheese dry noodles in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not have a code for salted egg yolk cheese dry noodles.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spicy salted egg cheese dry noodles is approximately 380 g, comprising a portion of cooked wheat noodles tossed in salted egg yolk sauce with chicken, melted cheese, and corn.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — cooked noodles (NDB 20110), salted duck egg yolk (USDA Branded / FAO/INFOODS SEA analogue), cheddar cheese (NDB 01009), cooked chicken breast (NDB 05064), sweet corn (NDB 11167), and butter (NDB 01001); weighted by estimated recipe proportions for a typical Singaporean salted egg yolk dry noodle dish.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Spicy Salted Egg Cheese Dry Noodles exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for cooked wheat noodles (~50% by weight), salted egg yolk (~10%), cheddar cheese (~8%), cooked chicken (~18%), corn (~8%), and butter/sauce (~6%). Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this dish type. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and mixed noodle dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles (GI ~52, Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) and salted egg yolk / cheese / protein components which lower the overall GI of the composite dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Salted Egg Cheese Dry Noodles. GI of 52 is estimated using cooked wheat noodles as the primary carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~52 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008). The addition of fat (salted egg yolk, cheese, butter) and protein (chicken) is expected to moderately lower the effective GI of the composite dish; GI 52 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (52 × 22.5 × 3.8) / 100 = 44.5 g, based on 380 g serving and 22.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1976 mg per serving) from salted egg yolk, cheese, and seasoning sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load per serving, which may exceed 80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal; requesting reduced sauce or sharing a portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) and high glycemic load per serving (~44.5 g) due to a large wheat noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish as the large noodle portion produces a high glycemic load per serving despite a moderate GI; reducing portion size, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitoring postprandial glucose response is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~3.9 g per 100 g; ~14.8 g per serving) and cholesterol (~95 mg per 100 g; ~361 mg per serving) from salted egg yolk, cheese, and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally, as the combination of high saturated fat from cheese and salted egg yolk and elevated dietary cholesterol may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels and cardiovascular risk; a serving provides approximately 74% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Salted Egg Cheese Dry Noodles (香辣咸蛋芝士干捞面) is a contemporary Singaporean fusion noodle dish that combines the rich, savoury umami of salted duck egg yolk with the creamy indulgence of melted cheese, served over a base of dry-tossed wheat noodles. The dish is prepared by boiling wheat noodles until al dente, then tossing them in a sauce made from cooked and mashed salted egg yolks, butter, chilli, and curry leaves to create a glossy, spicy, and intensely savoury coating. Toppings typically include sliced or shredded chicken, sweet corn kernels, and a generous layer of melted cheese, adding contrasting textures of tender protein, soft corn, and creamy dairy. The flavour profile is bold and complex — simultaneously salty, spicy, buttery, and umami-rich — with the salted egg yolk providing a characteristic sandy, granular richness that has become a signature of modern Singaporean food culture. This dish is a product of the salted egg yolk trend that swept Singapore's food scene in the 2010s, applied to the traditional dry noodle (gan lao mian) format. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~198 kcal per 100 g) with moderate protein (~9.8 g), significant fat (~8.2 g, of which ~3.9 g is saturated), and a moderate carbohydrate content (~22.5 g) from the noodle base. Sodium is notably high (~520 mg per 100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-moderate), but the glycemic load per full restaurant serving is high (~44.5 g) due to the large noodle portion. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, cholesterol, and glycemic load profile.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Spicy Salted Egg Cheese Dry Noodles exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data for the constituent ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy with cooked wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach and absence of a direct laboratory analysis for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy data; no direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry exists for this specific dish. Values should be treated as indicative estimates pending direct measurement. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for the constituent ingredients in the USDA SR Legacy source used for this estimation, and no direct measurement is available. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence across the component ingredients in the estimation basis. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as selenium content is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy sources used for the component estimation. 5. GI of 52 is a conservative analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles; the effective GI of the composite dish may be lower due to fat and protein content. 6. Halal status is marked false as salted egg yolk dishes in Singapore may use duck eggs and the restaurant has not been confirmed as halal-certified. 7. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed' as this is a composite noodle dish with multiple food groups represented. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g) / 100 = (52 × 22.5 × 3.8) / 100 = 44.5 g.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from promotional menu string '🇸🇬 Spicy Salted Egg Cheese Frenzy' to canonical English 'Spicy Salted Egg Cheese Dry Noodles'.
Corrected food_group from 'Dairy and eggs' (input) to 'Mixed' reflecting the composite nature of the dish.
Added all five mandatory Singaporean local-language names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Built comprehensive aliases list and aliases_by_language with full consistency verification.
Estimated per_100g nutrient values via weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy approved sources.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Estimated GI of 52 using University of Sydney GI Database analogue (cooked wheat noodles) and Atkinson et al. 2008.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving as 44.5 g based on 380 g serving size.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Wrote LLM-ready natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Set ontology codes as closest_parent with honest gap notes; no codes invented.
